title: Mango butter cold-process soap
subtitle: A rich conditioning bar with mango butter, olive oil, and coconut oil at 5% superfat
excerpt: Mango butter melts at body temperature and produces a bar with a light, non-greasy feel. Combined with olive oil for conditioning and coconut oil for lather and hardness, this recipe makes a bar well suited to dry and normal skin. The sweet orange essential oil is volatile at high temperatures -- add it at light trace.
sourceNotes: SAP values used in calculations: olive oil 0.134, coconut oil 0.190, mango butter 0.137, castor oil 0.128. 5% superfat applied. Reference: The Handcrafter's Companion, Anne L. Watson. Mango butter SAP value: SoapCalc and cosmetic formulation references.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Always run revised oil weights through a lye calculator before scaling -- do not simply multiply the lye weight.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cold-process soap requires a 48-hour rest in the mould before unmoulding, then a 4 to 6-week cure on a rack.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Cured bars keep for one year at room temperature on an open soap dish. Discard if bars develop a rancid smell or orange oily spots (DOS).

glossaryTerms.definition: The alkali that reacts with oils in cold-process soap making (saponification). Always add lye to water, never water to lye. Wear goggles, gloves, and ensure ventilation during the lye-water mixing step.

content.text: Mango butter is a solid at room temperature and melts readily in the oil phase. It contributes a slightly firmer bar than shea butter at the same usage rate, and its feel on skin is lighter and less occlusive. At 20% of the oil blend (100 g in a 500 g batch) it provides noticeable conditioning without dominating the lather. Handle

Lye causes chemical burns on contact with skin and eyes. Wear safety goggles and nitrile gloves throughout. Work in a ventilated area -- the lye-water mixture releases heat and fumes when first mixed. Add lye to water, never the reverse. Keep children and pets out of the workspace during the lye steps.

items.prepNote: 99% purity cosmetic-grade NaOH; calculated at 5% superfat
about 2.8% of oil weight; add at light trace

content.text: Wearing goggles and gloves, weigh 175 g of distilled water into the HDPE lye pitcher. Weigh the sodium hydroxide separately. Add the lye slowly to the water, stirring as you go. The mixture will heat to around 80 to 90°C and release fumes; do not breathe over the pitcher. Set aside to cool to 40 to 50°C.

Melt the coconut oil and mango butter together over low heat -- mango butter softens before coconut oil, so the two melt at a similar rate. Add the olive oil and castor oil. Allow the blend to cool to 40 to 50°C. Aim to have both the oil phase and the lye solution within 10°C of each other.

Pour the lye solution slowly into the oil phase, stirring as you pour. Use a stick blender in short bursts alternating with hand stirring until the mixture reaches light trace. Add the sweet orange essential oil at light trace and stir through. Note that sweet orange is a top note and will fade significantly over time in a finished bar -- this is expected and normal.

Pour into the silicone loaf mould. Cover and wrap in a towel to insulate. Leave undisturbed for 48 hours. Unmould and slice with the wire cutter. Place cut-side up on a rack in a ventilated space to cure.

Cure for a minimum of four weeks, ideally six. Test with pH paper at four weeks -- finished soap reads 9 to 10. The mango butter contributes hardness as the bar hardens, and the finished bar will be noticeably firmer than when first sliced.
